speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Murray Pantirer in Hillside, New Jersey. So you said that Schindler called all of you in and he talked to you. And do you remember anything that you want to share about that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you have-- you're going to be free, the war has ended. But I have a storage room with guns. So everyone-- only those who know how to use a gun, protect yourself in case some Germans will pass by and they want to kill you. And also he put on some people in charge to a storage space where there's goods, and everybody should get a package. And May 9 we were liberated. Five, six days later, with a few friends of mine, we were already in Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to Krakow. I knew that there's a federation, Jewish federation, on Dluga Street. But I went down to my own home, Jozefa. It was on the fourth floor we lived. And I saw still our furniture and our belongings. There was a woman with some children. And I told her-- she immediately starts shaking. I said to her, don't worry, I didn't come to-- to get the-- some of the furniture. I don't need it. But I'm going to write down a little note that the second son from [? Lezur ?] Pantirer had survived the war. And, by a miracle, if maybe the older brother, maybe some other-- if somebody survived, or some relatives, I will register myself in-- on Dluga Street in the Jewish, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Federation?

speaker: interviewee
text: --federation. She asked me if I would want a tea or something to drink. I said no. But she said, sit down. And she sent out her son to-- to bring the Polish militia. And she said something to him-- I didn't know what. And the guy said, this woman paid for everything, and don't make any troubles. I said, I didn't come to make any troubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed in Krakow for a few weeks. And I could only hear the Poles say 'Zyd do Palestyny'-- 'Jew go to Palestine.' And listen-- they said, they killed them? Look how many they are. And we were on-- but I was trying to sell some of the goods that I got.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also-- in Czechoslovakia, I was-- I went into a factory. They had those stones for the-- lighters for the-- flip lighter. And that was worth a lot of money. So I could sustain myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they were saying, we don't [? abide ?] by Jews. The anti-Semitism was unbearable. And I said to myself, I just finished camp-- concentration camp. I-- I lived through the war. Why-- what I'm going to sit in another dictatorship, another communistic country? I'm going to go to-- to Israel, a free country. So I took my knapsack. And I went to as far as Linz. I went to the Donau, the River Donau. I cross it. That was the Russians on one side. On the other side of the Donau was Linz. And I went there, to Linz. And there was a small DP camp. First it was called [? Hart ?] and then it was called Bindermichl.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bindermichl there were nice rooms, like a garden apartment-- a living room, a kitchen, and three bedrooms. And they put into every bedroom two boys. So there was nice-- quarters are nice. But I told them that I want to go to Palestine. So they said if I go to Graz-- Graz was occupied by the British. And I-- somehow, with a little German, a little Polish-- and I found-- always we're looking for a Jewish soldier who spoke a little Yiddish. And I said, I want to go to Israel. And they said, you need a certificate. You cannot go to Israel because you want to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I decided I'm going to go to Ame-- I was already with the Americans. I'm going to go to America. So I was told by the Americans if you had a mother or a-- or a father, or brothers, or sisters in America, you can come. But you are on the Polish quotas. And they put more salt on the wounds, because I had nobody. But one thing-- So, first of all, I found out that during this war I became an orphan twice. Once I'm orphaned without-- when they killed my father and mother in 1942. Then I was an orphan without a country. A person without a country is like a child without a father and mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one good thing-- when they asked me to register, I registered my date of birth. And they asked me when I was liberated. And I was liberated May 10. So I wasn't 20 years old. And, being no family, I was written up as an orphan. But in 1946, I met my wife in August. And in 1947, in January 5, we got married. And when we got married, the special-- then I don't-- I was no more an orphan. But this was the best thing that could happen to me. I met my wife's mother, probably you saw. And it's-- I should be a little bit mad, because for three and a half years I linger in the DP camp, but doing absolutely nothing. They didn't teach us a trade, they didn't send us to school, they just gave us some UNRRA packages. Some people start going to America, but, all those who had relatives-- brothers, sisters-- in 1948. And to Israel, I couldn't go, because they wouldn't let you in. They intercept you, and they send you to Cyprus. I didn't want to go to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: The British send you. And then when the independence of Israel, 1948, the war broke out, and I was really scared. I was the only Pantirer left. And I had a wife-- and a beautiful wife-- and I didn't want to take a chance. In the meantime, there was a consul in Salzburg. And I used to go down to Salzburg from time to time, doing some business. I will stop by-- give a can of-- a pack of cigarettes, a pack of choc-- bag of chocolate, and ask where are my-- she said, there's a quota coming out, 200,000. And you'll probably-- first, I was told to give up the Polish citizens to become a staatenlos. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Staatenlos'?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Staatenlos' means without a country. And chances are that I will be among the 200,000. And the fact is, she put me on top of the list. And in 1948, in January-- in December, I was from the first few that they were called out to come to America. In 1949, January 17, I was a few weeks in Bremerhaven. But then I came to America, to Boston Harbor. And from Boston Harbor they took us to New York by train. The first few days, I was in the HIAS.

speaker: interviewer
text: HIAS?

speaker: interviewee
text: HIAS. And what impressed me most is the tall buildings and everybody was driving a car. I said, are they all millionaires? I couldn't believe it. And they told me that any worker can buy himself a car. So I said, this-- this country's a good country for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a job in a shop. And after a few months, I was laid off because there-- there was no work no more. Should we--? Yeah? Uh-huh-- you were laid off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- it stays with me a few-- two things. That on Passover, I went to say Yizkor. The last day of Passover, you remember those-- the dead ones. So I was then on Cypress Avenue-- Rabbi Paretzky. And when you say the prayers  for Yizkor, before the prayers there was a United Jewish Appeal. And the United Jewish Appeal there was for those who were in camp and lived in huts in Israel, for $10,000 they could build them an apartment then. So there was quite a few people who gave $10,000, people who gave $5,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the end, I stood up and I said-- I was at the very end, at the back row; I was a survivor, a refugee-- and I said, I give $2. So everybody turned around and said, who's that big spender who gives $2? But-- and I got sweat, and my face got red that you could probably lit a cigarette. But I said to myself, dear God, that's all I can afford. If I afford-- can afford more, I give more. And the rabbi picked it up, and he said, I spoke to this man. He's a survivor from the Holocaust, and he's making $20 a week. And it struck-- it struck me once-- I was on Crotona Park. On a Sunday I used to go out to Crotona Park,. And the people who work in the factories-- [? furriers, ?] clocks operators. And there was an old guy who took a liking to me. And he said to me, why did you come to America? You were in the-- I know that you were in concentration camp. But now Europe going to be all communistic. You're not going to worry about a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I used to tell him, take me to the factory. Give me any job. I sweep floors, I wash, I do anything. He said, in America it's hard to make a living. But you live under communism, you would get a job and you would be OK. And I said to myself, how stupid. But the man doesn't know. And I was under one dictatorship already. Not to be under another dictatorship. I want to be in a free country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the following Sunday, I saw him in park washing his brand-new Pontiac. And in my thinking, I was sure that he's not making enough money during the week. Some millionaire hired him to wash his cars. And washing cars, I can do that, too. So I ran over to him, and I said, let me help you wash the car. And how much are you getting for washing a car? I-- I was thinking I'm going to have an enterprise, a business. The guy said, it's my car. I said, it's your Pontiac? You old fool! --in Yiddish, I said to him. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] In Russia, a general doesn't drive a-- a Pontiac. This is the-- that misunderstanding that people have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1950, I decided to start building. And thank god it was a good business. I dedicated my life for my family-- to my family. I actually had three choices when I-- the war finished. Either not to continue. To take my own life and say, I lost all the family, I have no strength to continue. And that would have been a mistake. Because with force you live and with force you must die. Life is not in your hands, it's in God's hand. Then I could  have married a non-Jewish girl or married and not have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of my friends-- not friends, but I knew them-- that they didn't circumcise their children. And we asked them, why don't you circumcise your children? He said, why should I bring up another generation for another to kill? And then that would've been even a bigger mistake, because the Nazis-- and there are plenty of Nazis-- will say, we gave them such a scare, we did-- we want to kill them all. Hitler give us orders to kill them all. But because the war finished six months earlier, we didn't have the opportunity to kill them, so they killed themselves. So I decided-- and especially in '49, when my son was born-- that first I'd give him a Jewish education. If he wants to change way of life, let him do it out of knowledge, not out of ignorance. And I-- what can I say to you? If the Jewish Federation of-- of New York would say to me, Murray Pantirer, you went through a lot. We couldn't help you during the war years, because we didn't know. We didn't understand. But now that you're in America, you don't have to do a thing anymore. That means the UJA, the Jewish Federation, will support you the rest of your life. How lucky that they didn't offer and lucky that I didn't ask for it. Because I would never have the guts to write down on paper all those wonderful things that happened to me. So I can say to you truly that in the building business, in the beginning, I work harder than in the concentration camp. The moral is that, even if they hold a gun in front of you, but if you don't want to work, you're not going to work. But for yourself, you work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I taught my children not to use the word 'hate.' And philosophy of my life is you're watching sometimes the marathon 20,000 people run from Staten Island. And when you come in number 1,000, you have a choice to say, gee, there's 1,000 people in front of me and to start blaming maybe the guy who gave me water hold me back a minute. Maybe I did this. Maybe the guy who run in front of me was tripping me. Maybe I was with afraid to trip. The rain, the wind-- you can start blaming yourself all you like-- night and being unhappy. But turn around for a few seconds, and you see 19,000 people in back of you, you should be thankful that-- that you cannot see with your own eyes that so many people. That does not mean that next year you shouldn't try to make it 500. But you should be very happy and pleased. Because to everyone that you complain that something is missing, there's hundreds of thousands of children who don't have what you have. That's my philosophy of life. And every day I hope I do something good. I try to be involved with a lot of things. And hope and pray that a Holocaust will never happen-- I'm sure it will not happen to the Jewish people, because they will not be-- they will be too [NON-ENGLISH]. When Samson-- Samson pulled out the pillars, if you want that for me, it will be that for everybody. So they-- they were being on guard. And that's why I make the tapes, to-- The most important thing-- if a bigot talks about Jews or the word I don't want to say it-- N-- or other people-- because there's always somebody who knocks somebody down-- he wants to kill them, believe him. Don't say he has only 10 people and he's crazy, because Hitler also started with 10 people. Believe him, and nip it in the bud. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wanted to say that listening to this whole tape I was amazed at how much you were able to remember. You were so young when the war started. You said you were 14. Dates, names of people-- how do you-- what do you attribute your ability to remember so much to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I study the Talmud. And I would always-- if they have an exam, I would do it by heart. I could even remember voices. After the war, I used to travel to Italy, to Austria, to certain camps-- where people were still in their pajamas because they were sick-- to walk around. And I will ask them for a name. And I will tell them, I saw your brother, I saw. I put together Mrs Zuckermann-- Mr Zuckermann's wife lived in Bindermichl with us. And I went to Italy. And I found out her room for-- for-- in a camp in Italy. I would remember names. I remember faces. And in business, I could rattle off hundreds of telephones without checking a number. Now I'm getting old. But thank  God for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. Is there anything else that you want to say on the tape to your-- to your children, to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will make it a separate tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: But is there anything else that you want to say now? Anything for the people who are going to be watching the video?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the people who are watching the video, one man does make a difference. And you always have to say, if you win a battle, what next? You don't have to have always your way. It's better to give than to receive. And a person is not-- came not in this world to be neutral. You came in this world to produce-- to give something to the world. And if you cannot give-- if, god forbid, you're sick or you're very poor-- then you have an obligation to ask for-- and that you should be helped. And no man should be big enough to say, I don't want to give. And no man should be small enough to say, I need a favor. Because in the world, they made money that is round. And round monies roll. Some days it rolls to your feet, and some days it can roll to my feet. And it's very important to say-- don't use the word, as I said to my children, 'hate.' Because when you start hating your room, and you start hating your friend, and then you hate your teacher, and then your mother, and everybody else. Live a life to be satisfying. You always reach out for a little bit more. But if you-- in America, there's a wonderful word-- 'I did my best.' After you know that you did your best-- in your job, and to your family, and to your fellow man-- then you have-- you did your best. And with that, you should be happy and satisfied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Pantirer, thank you very much for allowing us to interview you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can call the children now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Murray, some of your family is here today. And can you tell us who they are-- who's here visiting?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my left, my wife. And she's holding Elizabeth. I'm holding Elliot. Then is Marc, David, Nancy Pantirer, Elisa Pines, Justin Pines, and Nicole Pines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Elisa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And growing up with your parents, did they speak about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Not so much when we were young. They spoke more when we were a little bit older. Really pretty much when we got married, they-- they more talked about the war. They told us about what happened. And then we all came to realize what a terrible time these two had. But they were always smiling. They were always happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that that the way they raised you-- that their-- their experiences in the Holocaust had something to do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With how you-- how do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were overprotective. We didn't ride our bicycles. They were afraid for us to go to our friends' houses when we were young. And now I understand. So that's, I think, why I'm so overprotective of my children. It just happens. And our grandmother lived with us, so we were extra overprotective.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there something you'd like to say to your father and mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm proud of him. I love him. I love my mom. She's my best friend. I love Oskar Schindler for saving my dad. And they're the best. And I'm very, very lucky to have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Nancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're-- you're their daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the experience that you've had in the family, and with-- with raising your family, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew my family-- my in-laws-- I lived down the street, so I knew them for a long time. And I understood, as much as I could, what had happened, through Larry, my husband, who told me a lot that had happened, you know, the stories he had heard. And when I had my children, it made it more like it happened to them too, you know. So I understand that it made me probably-- every parent probably feels their children are so very precious. But the thought that they might not be here was even more apparent to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the grandchildren-- Marc? David?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is David.

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David. Uh-huh. Did you want to say something to grandparents and--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, OK. I'm very proud. And I'm very happy that they were able to survive. And that-- I'm very thankful that Oskar Schindler was there. And I'm glad that they're there, because now I'm here. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marc, anything you want to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're my grandparents. I love them. And I'm really happy they survived the war. I'm really thankful to Oskar Schindler. And I love them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nicole?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just love my grandparents so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Justin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very appreciative that Oskar Schindler could save my grandfather and that eventually would come out me and the rest of my cousins. And I love my grandparents very much. And I'm thankful to them and Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elliot, you want to say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: And anything-- say something to Grandpa and Grandma?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love them, and I'm thankful that Oskar Schindler saved them. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lizzie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lizzie?

speaker: interviewee
text: To [? Bob and Zadie. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: You love them, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're terrific grandparents. And you're a very lucky family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
